title: Non-Hermiticity-induced chirality imbalance of Weyl Landau levels
authors: Sachin Vaidya, Alaa Bayazeed, André Grossi Fonseca, Adolfo G. Grushin, Marin Soljačić, Christina Jörg
abstract: Weyl semimetals obey a global chirality constraint: the net chiral topological charge and any associated chiral spectral flow must vanish, as required by the Nielsen-Ninomiya theorem. Under magnetic fields, this constraint manifests through counter-propagating zeroth Landau levels associated with Weyl nodes of opposite chirality. Here, we experimentally demonstrate how non-Hermiticity can reshape this balance in a synthetic photonic Weyl semimetal. Using engineered gauge fields in one-dimensional multilayer structures, we realize both homogeneous and axial magnetic fields and directly probe the resulting Landau-level spectra. While a homogeneous field produces the expected chirality-balanced zeroth Landau levels, an axial field spatially separates the compensating chiral channels: co-propagating bulk pseudo-Landau levels carry one chirality, whereas the opposite chirality resides in boundary-localized surface states. We show that radiative boundary loss selectively suppresses these surface states, removing them from the long-lived observable spectrum and producing an experimentally accessible chirality imbalance. By reducing boundary loss, we recover the hidden chiral channel and reveal its surface-state origin. These results show that non-Hermiticity, present naturally in photonics, can control and relax fundamental chirality constraints in topological systems, enabling access to otherwise forbidden spectral responses.
summary: This research demonstrates that non-Hermiticity in photonic systems can manipulate topological constraints governing Weyl semimetals. By applying an axial magnetic field, the chiral balance is spatially separated into bulk and surface states. Crucially, radiative boundary loss selectively removes the surface states, creating an observable chirality imbalance that can be recovered by modifying the boundaries.
main_problem: The paper investigates how non-Hermiticity, inherent in open photonic systems, can induce and control an observable chirality imbalance in Weyl Landau levels, thereby bypassing fundamental topological constraints like the Nielsen-Ninomiya theorem.
main_result: By engineering an axial magnetic field and exploiting radiative boundary loss, the surface-localized chiral states are selectively suppressed, leading to an apparent net chirality imbalance in the long-lived bulk spectrum.
method: The study combines theoretical modeling using effective Hamiltonians and transfer matrix simulations with experimental measurements of light transmission through multilayer photonic structures.
model_or_system: The system is a synthetic photonic Weyl semimetal realized in multilayer Bragg stacks of Si/SiO2. The physics is governed by the 1D Maxwell eigenvalue equation, emulating 3D Weyl physics via synthetic momenta and engineered gauge fields (homogeneous and axial magnetic fields).
key_observables: Zeroth Landau level dispersion, chiral spectral flow, and the measured transmission spectrum showing the recovery of avoided crossings upon boundary modification.
important_parameters: The relative strengths of the homogeneous ($B$) and axial ($B_5$) magnetic fields, and the strength of the radiative boundary loss ($\eta$).
main_assumptions: The system is treated as an open, dissipative photonic structure where boundary loss dictates the observable spectral flow; the initial state assumes periodicity enforcing the theorem in the closed limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the unit cell structure, the transformation of Weyl nodes into Landau levels under different fields, and critically, the comparison of transmission spectra with and without a dielectric Bragg reflector, showing the re-emergence of surface states.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the theoretical framework of chirality constraints in Weyl systems, then demonstrating the effect of an axial field ($B_5$) leading to spatial separation of chiral channels, and finally showing that non-Hermitian boundary loss selectively removes the compensating surface states, thus inducing the imbalance.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO/Quantum Optics because it uses engineered photonic systems to simulate complex topological phenomena, demonstrating how dissipation (non-Hermiticity) acts as a controllable physical mechanism to modify fundamental quantum constraints.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of one unit cell of the photonic multilayer stack with alternating Si and SiO2 layers. (b) Band structure of frequency (𝜔𝑎/2𝜋𝑐) vs. the two synthetic momenta 𝑝and 𝑞, at 𝑘𝑦= 0, showing eight Weyl points between bands 2 and 3 with topological charges +1 (red) and -1 (blue). (c) Transfer-matrix simulation for 20 unit cells of the multilayer stack at 𝑞= 0, 𝑘𝑦= 0. Along this cut, four Weyl points with charges −1, +1, +1, −1 (from left to right) are visible.

FIG. 2. (a) The two oppositely charged Weyl points transform into dispersive zeroth Landau levels (0LLs) under an applied magnetic field. The Landau levels have opposite slopes under a homogeneous B-field and the same slope under an axial magnetic field (B5). Surface states are indicated by dashed light blue lines. (b) A schematic of the experimental measurement setup: broadband light from a halogen lamp illuminates the multilayer stack and the transmitted spectrum is recorded using a spectrometer. (c) Transfer matrix simulation of the spectrum for 𝑞= 0, 𝑘𝑦= 0 under a homogeneous B-field. The zeroth Landau levels inherit their chirality from that of the Weyl nodes shown in Fig. 2c. (d)...

FIG. 3. (a) Schematic of Landau levels under an axial magnetic field (B5) and boundary loss: in the transmission spectrum, the surface states (pale blue) are highly broadened and unobservable. Upon decreasing surface loss, they become long-lived and are visible as sharp resonances that hybridize with the zeroth Landau levels (red) in both transfer-matrix simulations (d) and the experiment (e). To reduce the loss, two bilayers of cladding have been added to the top of the multilayer stack, as shown in (b) and (c).
FIG. 4. Focused ion beam (FIB) cut through a typical multilayer stack revealing the different layers. The silicon layers appear dark gray while the silicon dioxide layers appear light gray.

FIG. 5. Competition between the 𝐵and 𝐵5-fields. (a) For 𝐵> 𝐵5 (𝛿= 0.3) the four zeroth Landau levels disperse in opposite directions, whereas for (c) 𝐵< 𝐵5 (𝛿= 0.8) they disperse in the same direction. (b) At the crossover point 𝐵≈𝐵5 (𝛿= 0.6), the outer two zeroth Landau levels collapse to Weyl points.
